title: Two Entrepreneurs Planning a Startup
summary: Alex and Jordan, two friends launching a food-tech startup together, work through their core business model, funding strategy, and how to divide roles and responsibilities.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: So I've been going over the numbers again, and I think we need to have a frank conversation about the business model before we go any further with the pitch deck.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: The subscription model we've been working with assumes sixty percent retention at twelve months. Every comparable company in the sector is reporting closer to thirty-five to forty. If we use our numbers in the pitch, we're going to lose credibility fast.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: I hear you, but I think our product differentiation justifies the assumption. We're not just another meal kit service — we have the dietary personalisation engine, which none of the incumbents have built properly.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: Maybe. But investors aren't going to take that on faith. They're going to look at sector benchmarks and push back hard. We need either to revise our projections to be defensible or to have a very compelling narrative about why we're genuinely different.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: What if we modelled both scenarios? A conservative case based on sector averages and an optimistic case based on our product features. That shows intellectual honesty while still making the bull case.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: That actually works well. Investors respect founders who acknowledge uncertainty rather than oversell. It might actually strengthen our credibility rather than undermine it.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: Exactly. Now, on the funding question — I've been thinking we should go to seed investors rather than trying to bootstrap further. We're at the point where the product is proven enough but we need capital to scale distribution.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: I agree on timing. My concern is dilution. If we raise a seed round now, we're probably giving away twenty to twenty-five percent at valuations that don't fully reflect the technology we've built.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: That's the trade-off in any early raise. But I'd rather have twenty-five percent of something that scales than a hundred percent of something that stagnates. Capital velocity matters more at this stage than equity preservation.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: Fair point. But let's make sure we're negotiating hard on valuation — I think we're worth at least three million pre-money, given the IP we've developed and the beta traction we have.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: Three million feels right to me. Actually, I'd anchor at three-five and expect to land somewhere around three. Anchoring high gives you room to move without ceding ground you didn't need to.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: Good strategy. Now the other thing we haven't properly sorted is the equity split between us. We've been vague about it and I think it needs to be nailed down before we bring investors in.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: Agreed. I've given it a lot of thought. I've been doing the technical build full-time for eight months. You've been part-time for four. I think a sixty-forty split in my favour is fair, at least to reflect that.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: I understand the logic, but I'd push back slightly. My network is what got us the pilot customers, and the commercial strategy has been entirely mine. The technical build is essential, but so is the go-to-market.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: That's fair. What if we did fifty-five forty-five for now, with a vesting schedule over three years? That way neither of us walks away in year one and leaves the other holding the bag.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: Fifty-five forty-five with vesting I can live with. And let's be explicit about roles going forward — you lead product and tech, I lead commercial and investor relations. Equal voices on strategy.

body.speaker: Jordan
body.text: That works. Honestly, having this conversation now rather than after a raise is exactly the right call. The last thing you want is a cap table dispute when you're trying to close a deal.

annotations.meaningVi: bộ slide thuyết trình cho nhà đầu tư
annotations.exampleEn: Their pitch deck clearly outlined the problem, solution, market size, and financial projections.

annotations.meaningVi: mô hình đăng ký — khách hàng trả tiền định kỳ để sử dụng dịch vụ
annotations.exampleEn: The subscription model provides predictable recurring revenue, which investors favour.

annotations.meaningVi: tỷ lệ giữ chân khách hàng — phần trăm khách hàng tiếp tục sử dụng dịch vụ
annotations.exampleEn: High customer retention is a key indicator of product-market fit.

annotations.meaningVi: sự khác biệt của sản phẩm — điều làm sản phẩm nổi bật so với đối thủ
annotations.exampleEn: Their product differentiation lay in the use of AI to personalise every meal recommendation.

annotations.meaningVi: các doanh nghiệp đã có chỗ đứng vững trên thị trường
annotations.exampleEn: The startup had a real opportunity to outmanoeuvre the incumbents by being more agile.

annotations.meaningVi: tiêu chuẩn ngành — các chỉ số tham chiếu trong cùng lĩnh vực
annotations.exampleEn: Their churn rate was half the sector benchmark, which impressed the investment committee.

annotations.meaningVi: dự báo có thể bảo vệ được — số liệu có cơ sở để bào chữa
annotations.exampleEn: Always use defensible projections in your pitch — investors will scrutinise every assumption.

annotations.meaningVi: kịch bản lạc quan — tình huống tốt nhất trong phân tích kinh doanh
annotations.exampleEn: In the bull case, the company reaches profitability by the end of year two.

annotations.meaningVi: tự lực tài chính — phát triển doanh nghiệp bằng nguồn vốn của chính mình
annotations.exampleEn: They bootstrapped the company for two years before seeking external investment.

annotations.meaningVi: nhà đầu tư vòng hạt giống — người đầu tư vào giai đoạn đầu của startup
annotations.exampleEn: Seed investors typically provide capital in exchange for an equity stake before the product is fully launched.

annotations.meaningVi: sự pha loãng cổ phần — tỷ lệ sở hữu giảm khi phát hành cổ phần mới
annotations.exampleEn: Each funding round causes some dilution, reducing the founders' ownership percentage.

annotations.meaningVi: tốc độ triển khai vốn — mức độ vốn được sử dụng để tăng trưởng
annotations.exampleEn: In a competitive market, capital velocity — how quickly you can deploy funds to gain market share — is critical.

annotations.meaningVi: bảo toàn quyền sở hữu cổ phần
annotations.exampleEn: Some founders prioritise equity preservation over rapid growth, preferring to raise less capital.

annotations.meaningVi: định giá trước khi gọi vốn
annotations.exampleEn: They negotiated a pre-money valuation of five million dollars before the seed round closed.

annotations.meaningVi: bắt đầu đàm phán từ mức cao để có không gian nhượng bộ
annotations.exampleEn: Anchoring high in a negotiation is a standard tactic that leaves room to compromise.

annotations.meaningVi: phân chia cổ phần — tỷ lệ sở hữu giữa các đồng sáng lập
annotations.exampleEn: The equity split was the most contentious part of the co-founder agreement.

annotations.meaningVi: lịch trình trao quyền sở hữu — cổ phần được trao dần theo thời gian
annotations.exampleEn: A four-year vesting schedule with a one-year cliff protects the company if a founder leaves early.

annotations.meaningVi: phải chịu trách nhiệm một mình khi đối tác rút lui
annotations.exampleEn: If one co-founder leaves without a vesting agreement, the other is left holding the bag.

annotations.meaningVi: chiến lược đưa sản phẩm ra thị trường
annotations.exampleEn: A strong go-to-market strategy is as important as the product itself.

annotations.meaningVi: tranh chấp về bảng phân bổ vốn cổ phần
annotations.exampleEn: A cap table dispute between co-founders can derail a fundraising process entirely.

annotations.meaningVi: sự trung thực về mặt trí tuệ — thừa nhận sự không chắc chắn một cách thành thật
annotations.exampleEn: Intellectual honesty about the risks in a business plan builds more trust than overconfidence.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional sentences for business projection
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong thảo luận kinh doanh, Conditional Type 1 ('If we raise a seed round now, we're probably giving away…') dùng cho tình huống khả năng xảy ra; Conditional Type 2 ('If we use our numbers in the pitch, we're going to lose credibility') dùng khi hậu quả được đánh giá là gần như chắc chắn. Sự lựa chọn giữa hai loại này phản ánh mức độ tin tưởng của người nói vào kết quả.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Business planning uses conditionals to project outcomes: 'If we use our numbers in the pitch, we're going to lose credibility fast' (certain consequence); 'If we raise a seed round, we're probably giving away twenty-five percent' (likely outcome). The choice of tense signals the speaker's degree of certainty — a key feature of strategic business discourse.

grammarNotes.title: Reporting speech in negotiation: 'I'd argue', 'I think', 'I'd push back'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong đàm phán, người nói thường dùng 'I'd argue that', 'I'd push back slightly' thay vì 'I disagree' để duy trì sự hợp tác. 'I'd' (would) ở đây thể hiện giả định có điều kiện, làm cho sự phản bác nghe nhẹ nhàng và thăm dò hơn. Đây là đặc trưng quan trọng của giao tiếp kinh doanh chuyên nghiệp ở cấp B2+.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In negotiation, speakers use 'I'd argue', 'I'd push back', 'I think' instead of blunt disagreement to signal openness while still holding a position. The modal 'would' makes the pushback conditional and tentative: 'I'd push back slightly' implies 'under these circumstances I would consider pushing back', which is softer than 'I disagree'. This is a key feature of professional negotiation language.

translationVi:
Alex: Vậy là tôi đã xem lại các con số, và tôi nghĩ chúng ta cần có một cuộc trò chuyện thẳng thắn về mô hình kinh doanh trước khi tiến thêm với bộ slide thuyết trình.

Jordan: Đồng ý. Mối lo ngại chính của bạn là gì?

Alex: Mô hình đăng ký chúng ta đang sử dụng giả định tỷ lệ giữ chân sáu mươi phần trăm ở mười hai tháng. Mỗi công ty tương đương trong ngành đang báo cáo gần ba mươi lăm đến bốn mươi phần trăm. Nếu chúng ta sử dụng số liệu của mình trong bài thuyết trình, chúng ta sẽ mất uy tín rất nhanh.

Jordan: Tôi hiểu, nhưng tôi nghĩ sự khác biệt sản phẩm của chúng ta biện hộ cho giả định đó. Chúng ta không chỉ là một dịch vụ hộp bữa ăn khác — chúng ta có động cơ cá nhân hóa chế độ ăn uống, mà không có đối thủ nào đã xây dựng đúng cách.

Alex: Có thể. Nhưng các nhà đầu tư sẽ không tin vào điều đó. Họ sẽ nhìn vào tiêu chuẩn ngành và phản bác mạnh mẽ. Chúng ta cần điều chỉnh dự báo để có thể bảo vệ được hoặc có một câu chuyện rất thuyết phục về lý do tại sao chúng ta thực sự khác biệt.

Jordan: Nếu chúng ta mô hình hóa cả hai kịch bản thì sao? Một trường hợp thận trọng dựa trên mức trung bình ngành và một trường hợp lạc quan dựa trên các tính năng sản phẩm của chúng ta. Điều đó cho thấy sự trung thực trí tuệ trong khi vẫn đưa ra kịch bản lạc quan.

Alex: Điều đó thực sự hiệu quả. Các nhà đầu tư tôn trọng những người sáng lập thừa nhận sự không chắc chắn thay vì bán quá mức. Thực ra nó có thể củng cố uy tín của chúng ta thay vì làm suy yếu nó.

Jordan: Chính xác. Bây giờ, về câu hỏi tài trợ — tôi đã nghĩ chúng ta nên đi đến các nhà đầu tư vòng hạt giống thay vì cố gắng tự lực thêm. Chúng ta đang ở điểm mà sản phẩm đã đủ chứng minh nhưng chúng ta cần vốn để mở rộng phân phối.

Alex: Tôi đồng ý về thời điểm. Mối lo ngại của tôi là sự pha loãng. Nếu chúng ta huy động vòng hạt giống bây giờ, chúng ta có thể phải từ bỏ hai mươi đến hai mươi lăm phần trăm ở mức định giá không phản ánh đầy đủ công nghệ chúng ta đã xây dựng.

Jordan: Đó là sự đánh đổi trong bất kỳ đợt huy động vốn sớm nào. Nhưng tôi thà có hai mươi lăm phần trăm của thứ gì đó tăng trưởng hơn là một trăm phần trăm của thứ gì đó đình trệ. Tốc độ vốn quan trọng hơn bảo toàn vốn cổ phần ở giai đoạn này.

Alex: Điểm hợp lý. Nhưng hãy đảm bảo chúng ta đàm phán cứng về định giá — tôi nghĩ chúng ta đáng ít nhất ba triệu trước khi gọi vốn, với IP chúng ta đã phát triển và lực kéo thử nghiệm chúng ta có.

Jordan: Ba triệu có vẻ đúng với tôi. Thực ra, tôi sẽ neo ở ba triệu rưỡi và kỳ vọng đạt khoảng ba triệu. Neo cao cho bạn không gian di chuyển mà không nhượng bộ điều bạn không cần phải nhượng.

Alex: Chiến lược tốt. Bây giờ điều khác chúng ta chưa sắp xếp đúng cách là phân chia vốn cổ phần giữa chúng ta. Chúng ta đã mơ hồ về nó và tôi nghĩ nó cần được chốt trước khi chúng ta đưa nhà đầu tư vào.

Jordan: Đồng ý. Tôi đã suy nghĩ rất nhiều về điều đó. Tôi đã làm việc xây dựng kỹ thuật toàn thời gian trong tám tháng. Bạn đã làm bán thời gian trong bốn tháng. Tôi nghĩ phân chia sáu mươi bốn mươi có lợi cho tôi là công bằng, ít nhất để phản ánh điều đó.

Alex: Tôi hiểu logic, nhưng tôi sẽ phản bác nhẹ. Mạng lưới của tôi là thứ giúp chúng ta có khách hàng thí điểm, và chiến lược thương mại hoàn toàn là của tôi. Việc xây dựng kỹ thuật là thiết yếu, nhưng chiến lược đưa sản phẩm ra thị trường cũng vậy.

Jordan: Điều đó công bằng. Nếu chúng ta làm năm mươi lăm bốn mươi lăm bây giờ, với lịch trình trao quyền ba năm thì sao? Như vậy không ai trong chúng ta rút đi trong năm đầu và để lại người kia phải gánh chịu một mình.

Alex: Năm mươi lăm bốn mươi lăm với trao quyền tôi có thể chấp nhận. Và hãy rõ ràng về vai trò đi tới — bạn dẫn sản phẩm và công nghệ, tôi dẫn thương mại và quan hệ nhà đầu tư. Tiếng nói bình đẳng về chiến lược.

Jordan: Được. Thành thật mà nói, có cuộc trò chuyện này bây giờ thay vì sau khi huy động vốn là quyết định đúng đắn. Điều cuối cùng bạn muốn là tranh chấp về bảng phân bổ vốn khi bạn đang cố gắng chốt một thỏa thuận.

questions.prompt: What specific problem does Alex identify with their current business model projections?
options: Their cost structure is unsustainable compared to competitors
Their assumed retention rate is far higher than what comparable companies achieve
Their product pricing is set too low to attract serious investors
Their technology is not differentiated enough to justify their valuation
questions.explanation: Alex says: 'The subscription model we've been working with assumes sixty percent retention at twelve months. Every comparable company in the sector is reporting closer to thirty-five to forty.'
questions.hint: Alex compares their assumption to a specific sector statistic.

questions.prompt: What does Jordan argue justifies their higher retention assumption?
options: Their brand recognition and marketing budget
Their lower price point compared to competitors
Their dietary personalisation engine that incumbents have not built properly
Their exclusive supplier relationships for premium ingredients
questions.explanation: Jordan says: 'we have the dietary personalisation engine, which none of the incumbents have built properly.'
questions.hint: Jordan points to a specific technical feature as their differentiator.

questions.prompt: What does Jordan propose to address the tension between their projections and sector benchmarks?
options: Use only conservative benchmarks and explain the shortfall
Model both a conservative and an optimistic case to show intellectual honesty
Revise the projections downward entirely and reduce the funding ask
Present only the optimistic case and prepare rebuttals for investor questions
questions.explanation: Jordan suggests: 'What if we modelled both scenarios? A conservative case based on sector averages and an optimistic case based on our product features.'
questions.hint: Jordan proposes showing two versions of the projections.

questions.prompt: Why does Alex say showing uncertainty might actually strengthen their credibility?
options: Investors prefer cautious founders who prioritise capital preservation
Investors respect founders who acknowledge uncertainty rather than oversell
It signals that the founders have done rigorous due diligence
It reduces the risk of legal liability if projections prove inaccurate
questions.explanation: Alex says: 'Investors respect founders who acknowledge uncertainty rather than oversell. It might actually strengthen our credibility rather than undermine it.'
questions.hint: Alex frames honesty as a form of strategic communication.

questions.prompt: Why does Jordan say the time is right to approach seed investors?
options: The team is complete and they have a full management structure in place
The product is proven enough but they need capital to scale distribution
Their runway is ending and they have no choice but to raise externally
A strategic investor has already made an informal approach
questions.explanation: Jordan says: 'we're at the point where the product is proven enough but we need capital to scale distribution.'
questions.hint: Jordan identifies a specific stage of product development and a specific need.

questions.prompt: What is Alex's concern about raising a seed round at this stage?
options: The legal costs of structuring the deal are too high
They might have to give up too much equity at valuations that don't reflect their technology
Seed investors typically require board control, which would constrain the founders
The deal could fall through if due diligence reveals weaknesses in their projections
questions.explanation: Alex says: 'My concern is dilution. If we raise a seed round now, we're probably giving away twenty to twenty-five percent at valuations that don't fully reflect the technology we've built.'
questions.hint: Alex's concern is about the percentage of ownership they would lose.

questions.prompt: How does Jordan respond to Alex's concern about dilution?
options: By arguing they should wait six months to raise at a higher valuation
By saying capital velocity matters more at this stage than equity preservation
By suggesting they limit the funding ask to minimise the equity given away
By proposing they seek non-dilutive grant funding instead
questions.explanation: Jordan says: 'Capital velocity matters more at this stage than equity preservation.' He also notes he'd rather have twenty-five percent of something that scales than one hundred percent of something that stagnates.
questions.hint: Jordan compares owning more of something small versus less of something large.

questions.prompt: What pre-money valuation does Alex want to anchor at in negotiations?
questions.explanation: Alex says: 'I think we're worth at least three million pre-money, given the IP we've developed and the beta traction we have.'
questions.hint: Alex mentions a specific figure and two reasons to support it.

questions.prompt: What negotiation tactic does Jordan suggest regarding the initial valuation they put to investors?
options: Present the lowest realistic valuation to show modesty and openness
Anchor at the exact valuation you want to achieve
Anchor high at three-and-a-half million to leave room to move without unnecessary concessions
Let the investor name their number first and respond from there
questions.explanation: Jordan says: 'I'd anchor at three-five and expect to land somewhere around three. Anchoring high gives you room to move without ceding ground you didn't need to.'
questions.hint: Jordan describes a deliberate strategy that involves starting above the target.

questions.prompt: Why does Alex say the equity split needs to be resolved before investors are brought in?
options: Investors will require a signed co-founder agreement as part of due diligence
Unresolved equity creates cap table disputes that can derail a fundraising process
Legal documents cannot be prepared until the equity percentages are finalised
Investors will reduce their valuation offer if they sense co-founder tensions
questions.explanation: While Alex calls for it to be 'nailed down before we bring investors in', Jordan later confirms this reasoning: 'The last thing you want is a cap table dispute when you're trying to close a deal.'
questions.hint: Jordan's final turn explicitly explains the reasoning.

questions.prompt: What is Jordan's initial proposal for the equity split, and what justification does he give?
options: Fifty-fifty, because both co-founders contribute equally
Seventy-thirty in Jordan's favour, reflecting the longer full-time commitment
Sixty-forty in Jordan's favour, reflecting eight months full-time versus four months part-time
Sixty-five thirty-five, weighted by the technical complexity of Jordan's contribution
questions.explanation: Jordan proposes 'a sixty-forty split in my favour' based on having been 'doing the technical build full-time for eight months' while Alex was 'part-time for four.'
questions.hint: Jordan gives a specific time-based argument for his proposal.

questions.prompt: What does Alex argue in response to Jordan's time-based equity argument?
options: He challenges the accuracy of the hours Jordan claimed to have worked
He argues that his network and commercial strategy are equally essential contributions
He points out that part-time work can be more valuable if focused on the right priorities
He suggests bringing in an external advisor to resolve the dispute objectively
questions.explanation: Alex says: 'My network is what got us the pilot customers, and the commercial strategy has been entirely mine. The technical build is essential, but so is the go-to-market.'
questions.hint: Alex points to two types of contribution that are not technical.

questions.prompt: What compromise does Jordan propose for the equity split?
options: Sixty-forty with a two-year vesting schedule
Fifty-five forty-five with a three-year vesting schedule
Fifty-five forty-five with no vesting but a performance review after twelve months
questions.explanation: Jordan proposes: 'fifty-five forty-five for now, with a vesting schedule over three years.'
questions.hint: Both the percentage split and the time period are specified.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the vesting schedule both agree to include?
options: To give investors the ability to buy out co-founders who underperform
To ensure neither co-founder walks away in year one and leaves the other holding the bag
To allow the equity split to adjust automatically based on contribution
To comply with legal requirements for registered company directors
questions.explanation: Jordan explains: 'That way neither of us walks away in year one and leaves the other holding the bag.'
questions.hint: The purpose is to protect both co-founders from an early departure.

questions.prompt: How do Alex and Jordan agree to divide their roles going forward?
options: Jordan leads all technical and commercial decisions; Alex focuses on investor relations only
Alex leads product and tech; Jordan leads commercial and investor relations
Jordan leads product and tech; Alex leads commercial and investor relations
Both lead all areas jointly with equal say on all decisions
questions.explanation: Alex proposes: 'you lead product and tech, I lead commercial and investor relations. Equal voices on strategy.'
questions.hint: Alex is the one proposing the role division in his turn.

questions.prompt: What phrase does Jordan use to describe why having this conversation early is the right call?
options: It builds mutual trust before the pressure of the fundraising process begins
The last thing you want is a cap table dispute when you're trying to close a deal
Sorting structure early is what separates successful startups from failed ones
Investors will ask about it anyway, so it's better to have an answer ready
questions.explanation: Jordan says: 'The last thing you want is a cap table dispute when you're trying to close a deal.'
questions.hint: Jordan uses a specific phrase about what you do NOT want during a fundraising process.

cloze.template: Alex: I've been going over the numbers, and I think we need to have a frank conversation about the business model before we go any further with the {{b1}}. Jordan: What's your main concern? Alex: The subscription model assumes sixty percent {{b2}} at twelve months. Every comparable company is reporting closer to thirty-five to forty. If we use our numbers in the pitch, we're going to lose {{b3}} fast. Jordan: I think our product {{b4}} justifies the assumption. We have the dietary personalisation engine, which none of the {{b5}} have built properly. Alex: Investors are going to look at sector {{b6}} and push back hard. Jordan: What if we modelled both scenarios? A conservative case and an optimistic case. That shows {{b7}} honesty while still making the bull case. Alex: Investors respect founders who acknowledge uncertainty rather than {{b8}}. Now, on the funding question — should we go to seed investors rather than continuing to {{b9}}? Jordan: I'd rather have twenty-five percent of something that scales than a hundred percent of something that {{b10}}. Capital velocity matters more at this stage than {{b11}}. Alex: Let's make sure we're negotiating hard on {{b12}} — I think we're worth at least three million pre-money. Jordan: I'd anchor at three-five. {{b13}} high gives you room to move without ceding ground you didn't need to. Alex: The other thing we haven't sorted is the {{b14}} split between us. Jordan: A sixty-forty split in my favour seems fair. Alex: My network is what got us the pilot customers, and the commercial strategy has been entirely mine. Jordan: What if we did fifty-five forty-five for now, with a {{b15}} schedule over three years? That way neither of us leaves the other holding the bag.

blanks.explanation: 'Pitch deck' is the presentation used to raise money from investors.
'Retention' is the percentage of customers who continue subscribing over time.
Using inflated projections would cause them to 'lose credibility fast' with investors.
'Product differentiation' refers to the features that make their product distinct from competitors'.
'Incumbents' are established players already in the market.
Investors use 'sector benchmarks' to evaluate whether a company's projections are realistic.
Jordan recommends 'intellectual honesty' — transparent acknowledgment of uncertainty.
'Oversell' means to make claims that are stronger than the evidence justifies.
'Bootstrap' means to fund the business from internal resources without external investment.
'Stagnates' means stops growing — Jordan prefers some dilution over a company that doesn't scale.
'Equity preservation' — protecting the founders' ownership percentage — is less important than growing fast.
Alex wants to negotiate hard on 'valuation' — how much the company is worth before the investment.
'Anchoring high' is the negotiation strategy of opening with a high number to create room to come down.
The 'equity split' is how ownership of the company is divided between co-founders.
A 'vesting schedule' ensures equity is earned over time rather than all at once.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Alex and Jordan show that co-founder dynamics — including equity division, role clarity, and honest communication about projections — are as important as the product itself. To what extent do you agree that the human and structural foundations of a startup matter more than the quality of the initial idea, and what evidence from the dialogue supports your view?

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: So I've been going over the numbers again, and I think we need to have a frank conversation about the business model before we go any further with the pitch deck.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Agreed.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: What's your main concern?

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: The subscription model we've been working with assumes sixty percent retention at twelve months.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Every comparable company in the sector is reporting closer to thirty-five to forty.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: If we use our numbers in the pitch, we're going to lose credibility fast.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: I hear you, but I think our product differentiation justifies the assumption.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: We're not just another meal kit service — we have the dietary personalisation engine, which none of the incumbents have built properly.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Maybe.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: But investors aren't going to take that on faith.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: They're going to look at sector benchmarks and push back hard.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: We need either to revise our projections to be defensible or to have a very compelling narrative about why we're genuinely different.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: What if we modelled both scenarios?

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: A conservative case based on sector averages and an optimistic case based on our product features.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: That shows intellectual honesty while still making the bull case.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: That actually works well.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Investors respect founders who acknowledge uncertainty rather than oversell.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: It might actually strengthen our credibility rather than undermine it.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Exactly.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Now, on the funding question — I've been thinking we should go to seed investors rather than trying to bootstrap further.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: We're at the point where the product is proven enough but we need capital to scale distribution.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: I agree on timing.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: My concern is dilution.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: If we raise a seed round now, we're probably giving away twenty to twenty-five percent at valuations that don't fully reflect the technology we've built.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: That's the trade-off in any early raise.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: But I'd rather have twenty-five percent of something that scales than a hundred percent of something that stagnates.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Capital velocity matters more at this stage than equity preservation.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Fair point.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: But let's make sure we're negotiating hard on valuation — I think we're worth at least three million pre-money, given the IP we've developed and the beta traction we have.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Three million feels right to me.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Actually, I'd anchor at three-five and expect to land somewhere around three.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Anchoring high gives you room to move without ceding ground you didn't need to.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Good strategy.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Now the other thing we haven't properly sorted is the equity split between us.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: We've been vague about it and I think it needs to be nailed down before we bring investors in.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Agreed.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: I've given it a lot of thought.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: I've been doing the technical build full-time for eight months.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: You've been part-time for four.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: I think a sixty-forty split in my favour is fair, at least to reflect that.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: I understand the logic, but I'd push back slightly.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: My network is what got us the pilot customers, and the commercial strategy has been entirely mine.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: The technical build is essential, but so is the go-to-market.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: That's fair.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: What if we did fifty-five forty-five for now, with a vesting schedule over three years?

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: That way neither of us walks away in year one and leaves the other holding the bag.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Fifty-five forty-five with vesting I can live with.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: And let's be explicit about roles going forward — you lead product and tech, I lead commercial and investor relations.

sentences.speaker: Alex
sentences.text: Equal voices on strategy.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: That works.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: Honestly, having this conversation now rather than after a raise is exactly the right call.

sentences.speaker: Jordan
sentences.text: The last thing you want is a cap table dispute when you're trying to close a deal.
